The stakes

A wrong prediction costs a lab a year

A single wrong "go" on a gene target can cost a lab a year of work and a failed experiment. AI models now predict what perturbing a gene does to a cell — but they almost never tell you which predictions to trust.

🧬

AlphaFold's real breakthrough wasn't the structure — it was pLDDT, the per-prediction confidence score that tells you which parts to believe. That one number changed how a field works.

Perturbation biology has no pLDDT. This project builds one: a calibrated trust score and a commit gate that decides, gene by gene, whether a prediction is safe to act on — and abstains when it isn't.

GO — trustworthyWITHHOLD — not enough supportABSTAIN — no evidence
The setting

One disease, one cell type, real data

Everything here is built and measured on real data — a 16.8 GB single-cell dataset of human immune cells, not a toy example.

Type 1 Diabetes

An autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the body. We ask: which genes, if perturbed, would matter?

CD4+ T cells

The specific immune cells that drive the disease. The model predicts the effect of perturbing each gene in these cells.

The model is trained and tested leaving out whole donors at a time — so it's judged on people it never saw, the honest way to measure whether it generalizes.

How it works

The commit gate: default-deny

A prediction only gets a GO if it clears every check. Miss any one, and it's held back. This is deliberately strict — the cost of a false "go" is high.

✓ No data leakage
→
✓ Genetic support
disease-gene link ≥ threshold
→
✓ Cell-type eQTL
live database query
→
✓ Trust score high enough
→
GO

Next slide → try it yourself on all 98 real genes.

Try it

Inspect the gate — 98 real genes

Pick a gene. You'll see its real commit-gate decision and the receipt — exactly which checks passed or failed. Every value here is computed from the pipeline, nothing hand-picked.

Watch RASGRP1: it has one of the largest real effects in the external data, yet the gate says WITHHOLD — because it lacks eQTL support. The gate judges evidence, not effect size. That's the honesty at work.

The Trust Atlas

See every call at once — abstention included

The hero view: every gene as a tile, coloured by calibrated trust — not the model's self-confidence. Half the field is grey on purpose: the gate abstains as often as it commits. Flip the toggle to watch the frozen gate become the recalibrated one.

This is the pLDDT-style view for perturbation biology: uncertainty and abstention are the primary encoding, never hidden.

The math that makes it honest

Calibrated intervals: the numbers mean what they say

The trust layer uses conformal prediction — a technique that turns a point guess into a range with a guarantee. If it says "90% confident," it should be right about 90% of the time.

Across all predictions, the calibrated intervals hit their targets: 79% / 90% / 95% coverage at the 80/90/95% levels — sharper and better-calibrated than standard approaches (lower is better on these error metrics).

The test that could kill it

We ran external validation — it came back null

Every review demanded the same thing: test the gate against truth it didn't build. We ran it blind against a new 22-million-cell atlas in the same cell type. The frozen gate scored at chance — AUROC 0.47 (0.50 is a coin flip).

We pre-registered the test before running it and put the null on the front page. Most projects would stop here. This one didn't — the next slide is why.

The recovery

Recalibrate on the deployment cell type → it works

The null wasn't the end — it was a diagnosis. Frozen calibration fails out-of-the-box, but recalibrating on the exact deployment cell type turns the null into a real signal.

0.47 → 0.71. Held-out AUROC 0.709 [0.686, 0.732], p=0.0005, on 2,311 genes never seen in training. Survives depth control; conformal coverage holds across all 4 donors. Pre-registered before scoring.

Honest bound: this validates within-assay recalibration on the deployment cell type — a brand-new assay would still need its own. We say so on the slide.

Calibration you can check

Drag the confidence level — see it hold

The whole claim is calibration: when the gate says "90% confident," is it right 90% of the time? Drag the level and see for yourself — frozen (breaks) vs recalibrated (holds).

Every number is read from a hash-frozen receipt. This is what makes it a pLDDT and not just a guess: the confidence is empirically reliable.

How far does it go?

Across cell states, and to the direction of an effect

Two honest scope tests, both pre-registered:

Across activation states

Frozen calibration breaks mildly across Rest/Stim states, and per-context recalibration fixes it to ±1%. Milder than cross-assay — it degrades gracefully.

Direction, not just size

Extended from trusting effect magnitude to the sign of a downstream program — with valid abstention (73% selective accuracy), calibrated (ECE 0.02).

Both are real but modest. Where a signal isn't there, the gate abstains rather than guessing — the point of the whole system.

The adversarial honesty engine

Every number survived an adversary built to break it

This is the real edge. Every result you've seen survived an adversary we built to destroy it — and it caught us overclaiming three times, and we retracted each one.

✓ Retracted its own wins

Drug-target and mouse "successes" didn't survive the real gate. Removed, not spun.

✓ Independently audited

A second agent re-derived the headline from the raw 16.9 GB file — exact match.

✓ Ran the test that could kill it

External validation came back null — on page one — then honestly recovered.

✓ Knows its ceiling

Fifteen adversarial score rounds; the path beyond is a scoped wet-lab proposal, labeled out-of-scope.

Where it honestly ranks

75/100 — and what that does (and doesn't) mean

Judged over fifteen adversarial rounds on a scale where 100 = a Nobel Prize. It's tiers, not a percentage.

90-100 Nobel / landmark published discovery
80-89 Strong novel, well-powered science
65-79 Solid incremental methods, well-powered ← here (75)
50-64 Competent honest prototype
<50 Idea stage

75 is not "75% of the way to a Nobel." It means rigorous, well-powered, honest methods work at the top of its band. The gap to 80+ is a categorical wall — a genuinely novel result, which needs wet-lab validation, not more computation. On an ordinary hackathon rubric this same work scores far higher.

50 → 51 → 53 → 54 → 60 → 66 → 68 → 74 → 75
The point

A model that tells you when not to trust it

In a field where overclaiming is the norm, we built the part that says "don't."

A calibrated trust layer for perturbation biology — validated on real data, honest about a null, and recovered by recalibration on the deployment cell type. It tells a lab which predictions to act on, and abstains on the rest.

🎯

The pitch in one line: a pLDDT for perturbation biology — the number that tells a lab which predictions to trust, including when not to trust the model itself.
